text: When a permanent resident or foreign national is currently in Canada, has valid legal status but is found to be inadmissible, what document is prepared first by the CBSA officer?

options.a: Section 44(1) Report
options.b: Section 44(2) Report
options.c: Section 44(4) Report
options.d: All of the above.

explanation: 44 (1) An officer who is of the opinion that a permanent resident or a foreign national who is in Canada is inadmissible may prepare a report setting out the relevant facts, which report shall be transmitted to the Minister.

text: Based on the facts in the previous scenario, please identify whether this statement is true or false: The decision of the Immigration Division will most likely find that Gustavo is inadmissible on the ground of misrepresentation and issue a 5-year exclusion order (s. 225(3) IRPR).

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

text: What types of removal order can an Immigration Division (ID) Member issue after an admissibility hearing? Select all that apply.

options.a: Deportation orders
options.b: Departure orders
options.c: Restraining orders
options.d: Exclusion orders

explanation: With a Departure Order, you must leave Canada within 30 days... With an Exclusion Order, you cannot return for one year (five years for misrepresentation). With a Deportation Order, you are permanently barred...

text: In the case that an Immigration Division (ID) Member makes a decision after an admissibility hearing that is not in favour of the applicant, which of the following statement is NOT true?

options.a: The Member must date and sign an order indicating the applicable provisions of the Act and provide a copy to the parties
options.b: The Member must suggest the permanent resident or foreign national seek legal advice
options.c: The Member must notify the permanent resident or foreign national of their right to appeal to the Immigration Appeal Division (IAD)
options.d: The Member must notify the permanent resident or foreign national if they do not have the right to appeal, their right to file an application for judicial review in the Federal Court

text: Mr. J is a citizen of Liberia, of Mandingo ethnicity. He arrived in Canada in 2019 and made a claim for refugee protection. He was arrested and held in one of the detention centers in Vancouver. Mr. J was found to be inadmissible to Canada on security grounds for being a member of an organization which has engaged in acts of subversion by force and terrorism. Which division of the Immigration and Refugee Board should be conducting the hearing?

options.a: Immigration Appeal Division
options.b: Immigration Division
options.c: Refugee Protection Division
options.d: Refugee Appeal Division

explanation: At the request of the CBSA or the IRCC, the Immigration Division (ID), conducts admissibility hearings...

text: Which of the following are concepts of the RIBIC Factors?

options.a: Seriousness of the offence leading to the Removal Order
options.b: Establishment in Canada
options.c: Family support available to the individual
options.d: All of the above

explanation: the Ribic factors are: a. the seriousness of the offences... b. the possibility of rehabilitation; ... f. potential foreign hardship...

text: Frank, a Canadian citizen, wanted to sponsor Krissy as his common-law partner. Krissy is an international student. Therefore, they filed the application under the Spouse or Common-Law Partner in Canada category. Unfortunately, they received a refusal letter five days ago and have asked you to consult on their case to appeal the decision. Based on the information you have, what information would you provide to them?

options.a: They may not appeal the decision as the Spouse or Common-Law Partner in Canada Class application is not eligible for an appeal.
options.b: They have only ten days to submit the Notice of Appeal.
options.c: They may not appeal the decision as Krissy is an international student in Canada.
options.d: They have only 25 days to submit the Notice of Appeal.

explanation: If the application was for an inland spousal sponsorship, there is no appeal to the IAD. Instead, a judicial review of the decision can be filed with the Federal Court of Canada.

text: A client contacts you for a sponsorship appeal for their spouse who was recently found guilty of an offence in their home country which carried a maximum term of imprisonment of 6 years. If that same offence was committed in Canada, it would carry a maximum term of imprisonment of at least 10 years. Would the client have sufficient grounds for appeal? Select the best response.

options.a: No, as the spouse would likely be found inadmissible due to serious criminality
options.b: No, as the spouse would likely be found inadmissible due to major criminality
options.c: No, as the spouse would likely be found inadmissible due to criminality
options.d: No, as the spouse would likely be found inadmissible due to organized criminality

explanation: You cannot appeal if the person you sponsored was found inadmissible ... committing or being convicted of a crime outside Canada that would be punished in Canada by a maximum prison term of ten years or more.

text: When filing a sponsorship appeal, an appeal may be filed because errors may have been made in:

options.a: Fact
options.b: Law
options.c: Mixed Fact and Law
options.d: All of the above

explanation: The IAD examines cases before it for possible errors in law, in fact, and mixed law and fact, or for failure to observe a principle of natural justice.

text: For the purpose of filing a sponsorship appeal, what timeline must be adhered to for a sponsor to file for appeal with the Immigration Appeal Division (IAD)?

options.a: No later than 15 days from the date of the Immigration Division (ID) hearing
options.b: No later than 30 days after receiving the refusal letter to appeal to the IAD
options.c: No later than 45 days from the date a Sponsor received the written reasons for the Immigration Division (ID) decision
options.d: No later than 30 days from the date of the Immigration Division (ID) hearing

text: For the purpose of filing an appeal of a removal order at the Immigration Appeal Division (IAD), your client must have received a removal order if they are:

options.a: A Vulnerable Person or a Person in Need of Protection, Refugee or Permanent Resident who lost their Permanent Resident status
options.b: A Temporary Resident, Foreign National or Failed Refugee Claimant or a Person in Need of Protection
options.c: A Sponsored Family Class Member, Failed Refugee Claimant or Person in Need of Protection or a Permanent Resident who failed to meet their residency obligation
options.d: A Permanent Resident of Canada, a Foreign National with a Permanent Resident visa, or a Refugee or Protected Person

explanation: You can appeal if you received a removal order and you are: a permanent resident of Canada, or a foreign national with a permanent resident visa, or a Convention Refugee or Protected Person.

text: In which of the following situations is a refugee applicant required to submit a police certificate from the country which they have fled to the RPD?

options.a: When the country they have fled from is not listed as a designated safe country
options.b: When the country they have fled from was not part of the UNHCR
options.c: When the claimant did not have time to get the police certificate due to expedited travel arrangements
options.d: The claimant does not need to present a police certificate

explanation: Refugees are not required to submit police certificates from their country of origin.

text: A person (generally a refugee claimant) who was part of a group of persons smuggled into Canada that the Minister has designated as an irregular arrival is known as a...

options.a: Designated irregular arrival
options.b: Designated foreign national
options.c: Safe third country national
options.d: Mandatory inadmissible claimant

explanation: The Minister may designate as an irregular arrival the arrival in Canada of a group of persons... rendering them designated foreign nationals.

text: Foreign nationals making a refugee claim at the port of entry must undergo an examination by the CBSA officer in order to determine which of the following:

options.a: Identity
options.b: Inadmissibility
options.c: Eligibility
options.d: All of the above

explanation: When you make your refugee claim, you will speak with a CBSA officer. They ask questions to establish identity, see if you can make a claim (eligibility), and determine inadmissibility.

text: What is the time limit for providing the Basis of Claim Form if Refugee Claim is made at port of entry?

options.a: 10 days
options.b: 15 days
options.c: 7 days
options.d: 45 days

explanation: You must send your BOC form to the RPD within 45 calendar days. (It is no longer 15 days!!!)

text: In the context of refugee law, who has the burden of proof regarding whether a claim is eligible for referral to the Refugee Protection Division (RPD)?

options.a: The CBSA officer at the port of entry
options.b: The CIC officer at an inland office
options.c: The Minister of IRCC
options.d: The refugee claimant

explanation: (1.1) The burden of proving that a claim is eligible to be referred to the Refugee Protection Division rests on the claimant.

text: Which of the following statements accurately describes the principle of non-refoulement?

options.a: This principle prevents Canada from returning a refugee to their country of origin if their life is endangered due to a natural disaster or armed conflict.
options.b: This principle prevents Canada from removing refugee claimants who are guilty of serious non-political crimes until a final determination is made regarding their refugee claims.
options.c: This principle grants refugee claimants the right to access the court system and to have legal representation.
options.d: This principle prevents Canada from returning a refugee to the country where they face a risk of persecution.

explanation: You cannot be deported or sent back to a country in which your life or freedom may be in danger on account of your race, religion, nationality, membership of a particular social group or political opinion.

text: Which of the following will help an applicant qualify under the Convention Refugee definition?

options.a: He has good reason, backed up by experience, to believe his government will not protect him from the threats of persecution he has experienced.
options.b: The claimant is concerned that he will face discrimination, although he cannot point to any specific reason for that concern.
options.c: The claimant does not want to move to another part of his country to avoid persecution because he has always lived in the province he resides in.
options.d: The claimant is afraid that if he returns home he will face persecution.

explanation: IRPA Section 96 ... well-founded fear of persecution...

text: Which one of the following points will assist a claimant in succeeding at a refugee hearing?

options.a: If some of the documents in evidence were not completely consistent with the details given in the Basis of Claim form.
options.b: If the claimant did not come to Canada right away but passed through the United States first.
options.c: If the claimant had to return to his country after he left.
options.d: If the claimant sought protection right away upon arrival in Canada.

explanation: Delay in making a claim to refugee status is not a decisive factor in itself... Promptness assists credibility.

text: Which of the following is NOT TRUE in joining or separating claims at the RPD?

options.a: Claims for refugee protection are automatically joined in the case of a claim of a claimant to a claim made by the claimant's spouse or common-law partner always and in every situation.
options.b: Applications to vacate or to create refugee protection are joined if the claims of the protected persons were joined.
options.c: A party may make an application to the Division to separate claims or applications to vacate or to cease refugee protection that are joined.
options.d: A party who makes an application to join or separate claims, must do so in accordance with the Rules of the RPD.

explanation: The Division must join the claim... unless it is not practicable to do so.

text: Consider the Internal-Flight Alternatives (IFA) for Maria. Can Maria go to another city to flee her abusive relationship and get away from her husband Carlos?

options.a: Maria has no money or job to relocate to another city in El Salvador so an IFA is not an option.
options.b: Maria has no community connections in any other city and is not able to survive with 3 children by herself so an IFA is not an option.
options.c: El Salvador is a small country, making it very difficult for Maria to hide with her 3 children and will likely be easily be found by Carlos so an IFA is not an option.
options.d: Maria has no relatives in other cities in El Salvador so an IFA is not an option.

explanation: The Board must be satisfied there is no serious possibility of persecution in the IFA, and it must not be unreasonable to seek refuge there. Proximity defeats the IFA.

text: What is the function of the Refugee Protection Division (RPD)?

options.a: Considers claims for Refugee and Protected Status
options.b: Makes a final decision on refugee appeals
options.c: Have jurisdiction over sponsorship appeal decisions
options.d: Unsure

text: A very anxious client comes to your office for a consultation. He has been in Canada for about two years... (Venezuelan engineer fearing persecution). What are his options?

options.a: Express Entry profile.
options.b: He has a potential case of refugee based on his political opinion, so he can apply for Canada's protection, while his family changes status to visitors and wait for a positive decision of his case, which then will allow him to sponsor them.
options.c: All the family can apply for refugee protection, based on his political opinion and their potential risk at the hands of those who are persecuting him.
options.d: He has no choice but to return to Venezuela...

explanation: The definition of a Convention refugee states that a claimant's fear of persecution must be 'by reason of' one of the five enumerated grounds.

text: A potential client comes to your office wanting you to represent her and her family in their refugee process... They just received a notice to appear for a hearing in 20 calendar days... What do you do?

options.a: You refuse to take the case.
options.b: After explaining the client that having their forms is necessary and that there are only 10 days to submit their translated evidence, you propose them that... you will make an application to the RPD explaining that you were just recently hired and request for a postponement of the hearing to give time to translate evidence and submit on time.
options.c: You decide to represent the clients explaining them that during the hearing you will make an oral application.
options.d: You decide to represent the clients and tell them not to worry, that the day of the hearing you will make an application for adjournment.

text: A client states that a year ago he withdrew his in-Canada refugee claim prior to a hearing. He explains that he did not return to his home country and that he had to go to Europe to be with his mother during a high-risk surgery. He is now in Canada again. What should you tell him?

options.a: He can submit an application to re-open the previous claim.
options.b: He can make a new claim.
options.c: If his claim is referred to IRB, the Minister will likely intervene.
options.d: He is not eligible to make a new refugee claim.

explanation: If you withdraw your claim, you will not be permitted to make another claim for refugee protection in Canada.

text: The IRB Chairperson Guideline contains procedural accommodations with respect to vulnerable persons claiming refugee protection. Which of the following modifications to the refugee process might be allowed? Please select all that apply.

options.a: Allowing a support person to participate in a hearing
options.b: Varying the order of questioning
options.c: Providing a panel and interpreter of a particular gender
options.d: Possibility of making a positive decision based on humanitarian and companionate factors related to vulnerability
options.e: Allowing any other procedural accommodations such as expedited scheduling, providing an oral decision and/or decision without a hearing

explanation: Guideline 10.2 lists these accommodations (excluding H&C factors which are IRCC jurisdiction, not RPD).

text: In which of the following situations can Counsel use 'Objection rule' when a refugee claimant is being questioned by the Board Member?

options.a: Members may ask any questions in order to clarify the evidence and to disclose their concerns, even making inappropriate comments or demonstrating an unfavorable attitude.
options.b: When the Board Member subjects the claimant to constant interruptions, animosity or offensive remarks.
options.c: When the tone and content of questions by the Board Member are not judicious.
options.d: All of the above (meaning b and c justify objection)

text: Not all claims brought forward by women are specifically gender-related. Which of the cases below can be considered a gender-related persecution case?

options.a: If the claim relies on one of, or any combination of, the five enumerated grounds of the Convention Refugee definition
options.b: Women who fear persecution solely for reasons pertaining to kinship
options.c: Women who fear persecution resulting from certain circumstances of severe discrimination on grounds of gender or acts of violence either by public authorities or at the hands of private citizens
options.d: All of the above

text: For the purpose of filing an appeal, the Minister can appeal the Refugee Protection Division (RPD) decision to accept claimant's refugee protection claim, unless:

options.a: Claimant is a designated foreign national...
options.b: Claimant is a designated foreign national; Claimant made his/her claim at a land border with the United States... or Claimant's claim was referred to the RPD before the relevant provisions of the new system came into force in December 2012.
options.c: Claimant is a designated as a vulnerable person...
options.d: Claimant is a designated foreign national...

explanation: The Minister cannot appeal if you are a designated foreign national, made a claim at a land border exception to STCA, or referred pre-Dec 2012.

text: Xiu is a 60-year-old female citizen of Indonesia who currently has no family members residing in her country. She has received a negative decision from the Refugee Protection Division (RPD), in which the Member found that the claimant has a viable Internal Flight Alternative (IFA) in Indonesia. Which of the following arguments about the IFA are the most likely to be successful upon appeal to the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD)?

options.a: The negative impact the RPD decision has on any children of the claimant
options.b: The unlikelihood of the claimant reaching the IFA without undue risk to her life
options.c: The inability of the claimant to legally remain in the IFA area
options.d: The young age of the claimant

text: If a Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) claimant provided new evidence along with their Appellant Record, it is certain that a RAD hearing will be scheduled.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

explanation: A RAD hearing will be held only if the evidence shows a serious issue about credibility, is central to the decision, and would provide a good reason to allow or reject the claim.

text: What is the time limit for submitting the Notice of Appeal and Appellant Record to the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD)?

options.a: 15 days after receiving the RPD decision for Notice of Appeal and 45 days for Appellant Record.
options.b: 15 days after issuing the RPD decision for Notice of Appeal and 45 days for Appellant Record.
options.c: 15 days after receiving the RPD decision for Notice of Appeal and 30 days for the Appellant Record.
options.d: 15 days after receiving the RPD decision for Notice of Appeal and Appellant Record.

text: No appeal may be made in respect to a decision of the Refugee Protection Division (RPD) allowing or rejecting the claim for refugee protection of individuals who arrive as part of a Designated Foreign National.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

text: Appeals to the RAD are reviewed and decided by a single member unless the Chairperson is of the opinion that a panel of three members should be constituted.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

explanation: Proceedings are normally conducted by a single member. However, the Chairperson has the authority to constitute a panel composed of three members...

text: The Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) does not have jurisdiction to reopen on any ground - including a failure to observe a principle of natural justice - an appeal in respect of which the Federal Court has made a final determination.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

explanation: Section 171.1 explicitly states the RAD does not have jurisdiction to reopen once the Federal Court has made a final determination.

text: The Refugee Protection Division has jurisdiction to consider decisions of the Convention Refugee Determination Division that are set aside by the Federal Court, the Federal Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court of Canada and shall dispose of those matters in accordance with the provisions of IRPA.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

explanation: Section 198 grants the RPD this exact jurisdiction.

text: Sara receives a decision from the Refugee Protection Division (RPD) that her claim for refugee protection was rejected because the claim has no credible basis or was manifestly unfounded. What should your next step be? Select the best response.

options.a: File a notice of appeal to Refugee Appeal Division.
options.b: Advise the client that there is no right of appeal and that their only recourse is a judicial review at the federal court.
options.c: Send an application for Humanitarian and Compassionate consideration.
options.d: Complete a Pre-removal Risk Assessment.

explanation: You can't appeal the RPD decision if the decision says your claim has no credible basis or is manifestly unfounded.

text: For the purpose of IRBs Guideline anyone may file a complaint against an IRB Member about conduct that is believed to be contrary to which of the following?

options.a: The Code of Conduct for Members IRB
options.b: The Immigration and Refugee Protection Act
options.c: The Immigration and Refugee Protection Regulations
options.d: b and c

explanation: A complaint must be about the conduct of a member which is believed to be contrary to the standards of conduct set out in sections 9 to 15 of the code.

text: For the purpose of IRBs Guideline, the term 'vulnerable persons' is defined as which of the following?

options.a: Individuals whose ability to present their cases before the IRB is severely impaired (e.g., the mentally ill, minors, the elderly, or victims of torture).
options.b: Individuals committed to a mental ward/mental hospital whose ability to process information is severely or totally impaired by use of prescription or non-prescription drugs, or substances.
options.c: Individuals who have suffered severe mental trauma due to beating, torture, or an accident.
options.d: Children or their parents who are victims of sexual, mental, physical, domestic or psychological abuse, war situations and/or military conflicts.

text: A person who is the subject of an appeal who does not provide a document or written submissions with the appellant's record, respondent's record or reply record must not use the document or provide the written submissions in the appeal unless allowed to do so by the Division.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

text: In deciding whether to allow an application for a person who is the subject of an appeal that did not provide a document or written submissions with the appellant's record, respondent's record or reply record, the Division must consider only relevant factors... (a) the document's relevance and probative value; (b) any new evidence the document brings to the appeal; and (c) whether the person who is the subject of the appeal, with reasonable effort, could have provided the document...

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

text: If a Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) claimant provided new evidence along with their Appellant Record, it is certain that a RAD hearing will be scheduled.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

text: No appeal may be made in respect to a decision of the Refugee Protection Division (RPD) allowing or rejecting the claim for refugee protection of a designated foreign national.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

text: In the case of a proceeding before the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD), a decision of a panel of three members of the RAD has, for the Refugee Protection Division (RPD) and for a panel of one member of the RAD, the same precedential value as a decision of an appeal court has for a trial court.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

text: Mr. Kaur's refugee protection application was rejected by the RPD member alleging that the application has no credible basis or is manifestly unfounded. As his next step, Mr. Kaur can appeal the RPD decision to the RAD.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

explanation: You cannot appeal the RPD decision if it says your claim has no credible basis or is manifestly unfounded.

text: The Refugee Protection Division has jurisdiction to consider decisions of the Convention Refugee Determination Division that are set aside by the Federal Court, the Federal Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court of Canada and shall dispose of those matters in accordance with the provisions of IRPA.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

text: When does the Immigration Division first review the reasons for detention after an individual has been detained by CBSA?

options.a: Within 7 days
options.b: Within 30 days
options.c: Within 1 day
options.d: Within 2 days

explanation: A detained person has the right to a detention review hearing within 48 hours or as soon as possible afterward.

text: If a Foreign National or Permanent Resident is still not released within the first Immigration Division review for the detention, when does the Immigration Division review the reasons for detention again?

options.a: 7 days
options.b: 30 days
options.c: 1 day
options.d: 2 days

explanation: If the detained person is ordered to stay in detention, they will have another detention review within 7 days.

text: Before the admissibility hearing, Gustavo is told by his immigration consultant that 'The Immigration Division Member will present evidence and argue that you (Gustavo) have breached immigration laws and that you are therefore, inadmissible on the grounds of misrepresentation.' Is the statement made by the immigration consultant true or false?

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

explanation: A CBSA representative will explain to the member what the CBSA believes you are inadmissible to Canada.

text: Which of the options below are NOT correct in regards to this statement: The decision of the ID Member to issue an order to release a Person Concerned is...

options.a: Final
options.b: Final If there are appropriate conditions of release set out along with an order to release
options.c: Final, but if the Representative (RCIC) doesn't agree, they can seek Stay of Release
options.d: Final, but if Minister's counsel does not agree, they may seek an advice of Department of Justice and CBSA litigation Management on whether to apply for stay of release and/or judicial review

explanation: The RCIC cannot seek a stay of release. The Minister of Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness can disagree with the IRB’s decision to release someone.

text: An RCICs closing submission at an admissibility hearing should include the following. Select the most correct answer.

options.a: Present the position based on the evidence that was introduced during the hearing
options.b: Present the position based on the evidence that was already introduced and any new facts of the case at hand
options.c: Both a and b are acceptable during the closing submission
options.d: both a and b are not acceptable during the closing submission

explanation: Closing submissions are meant to summarize and argue based on the evidence that was already introduced on the record. You cannot introduce new facts during closing submissions.

text: Who is permitted in the hearing room at an admissibility hearing for a Person Concerned who is not a refugee claimant? Select all that apply.

options.a: Person concerned
options.b: Members of the public who do not know Person Concerned
options.c: Sibling of the Person concerned who is a Canadian citizen and is not testifying
options.d: Friend of the person concerned who is not a Canadian citizen and will be testifying
options.e: Expert Witness who will be testifying

explanation: The IRB will generally exclude witnesses from the hearing room before they testify... However, there are exceptions...

text: The head office of ID Western Region is located in:

options.a: Edmonton
options.b: Calgary
options.c: Alberta
options.d: Vancouver

explanation: All related documentation for proceedings must be sent to the Vancouver office.

text: What is the time limit for an applicant to provide documents required for a hearing before the Immigration Division (ID)? Select the best response.

options.a: 5 days
options.b: 10 days
options.c: 20 days
options.d: 30 days

explanation: In all other cases (besides 48-hour or 7-day reviews), at least five days before the hearing.

text: How do you address the decision maker in a hearing before the Immigration Division (ID):

options.a: My lord
options.b: Mister
options.c: Madam or Sir
options.d: Your Honour

text: Gino sponsored his spouse, Sherry, under the Outside of Canada Spousal Sponsorship category. His application was refused and he decided to appeal to the Immigration Appeal Division (IAD). What is the time frame to file a Notice of Appeal?

options.a: 30 days
options.b: 20 days
options.c: 60 days
options.d: 5 days

explanation: You must provide the Notice of Appeal to the IAD so that it is received no later than thirty (30) days after you received the IRCC refusal letter.

text: What is the timeframe to submit Disclosure Documents prior to an Immigration Appeal Division (IAD) hearing date?

options.a: 30 days
options.b: 20 days
options.c: 60 days
options.d: 5 days

explanation: In most cases, the IAD and the Minister’s counsel must receive your disclosure... no later than 60 days after you have received the appeal record.

text: What is the correct order of the stages of filing an Appeal before the Immigration Appeal Division (IAD): i. File the Notice of Appeal, ii. Set a hearing date with IAD Registry, iii. Wait for the Appeal Record, iv. Submit Disclosure documents.

options.a: i, iii, iv, ii
options.b: i, ii, iii, iv
options.c: ii, i, iv, iii
options.d: iv, iii, ii, i

explanation: File notice of appeal → Wait for Appeal record → Submit disclosure → Schedule hearing

text: Kamish comes into the RCICs office with all the requested documentation 23 days before the hearing date. Has Kamish provided the documents before the deadline?

options.a: No, as per Section 33 (1), the documents must be provided no later than 10 days before the hearing.
options.b: No, as section 33 (3), the documents must be provided no later than 15 days before the hearing.
options.c: Yes, as per Section 30 (3), documents must be provided no later than 20 days before the hearing.
options.d: No, as Section 27, the documents must be provided no later than 30 days before the hearing.

explanation: Documents must be provided no later than 30 days before the hearing (Section 27).

text: Imelda was a Permanent Resident of Canada. She was issued a Removal Order for misrepresenting... Is Minister's Counsel's argument correct?

options.a: Yes, Minister's Counsel is correct because it is one of the exceptions under Stay of Removal Orders
options.b: Yes, Minister's Counsel is correct because Imelda is a Convention refugee or a person in need of protection
options.c: No, Minister's Counsel is incorrect because Imelda meets the RIBIC Factors
options.d: No, Minister's Counsel is incorrect because a Stay of the Removal Order is not appropriate and the appeal should be allowed

explanation: The stay does not apply to a person who is referred to in section F of Article 1 of the Refugee Convention.

text: Alejandro, a Permanent Resident of Canada... what would be the first step that you would take to prevent his removal from Canada?

options.a: File an appeal with the Immigration Appeal Division
options.b: File a hearing with the Immigration Division
options.c: File an application for leave and judicial review with the Federal Court of Canada
options.d: Unsure

explanation: You can appeal if you received a removal order and you are a permanent resident of Canada.

text: Jane Doe wants to sponsor her husband... What would you recommend to her?

options.a: Jane may file for an appeal because outside-Canada spousal cases may proceed with the appeal despite a refusal due to misrepresentation
options.b: Jane's best option is filing for judicial review as a refusal due to misrepresentation prevents them from filing an appeal to IAD
options.c: Jane's best option is applying from scratch...
options.d: Since the refusal is due to misrepresentation, her spouse is inadmissible to Canada for five years.

explanation: The IAD may still accept appeals in cases of misrepresentation if the sponsored person is a spouse, common-law partner or child.

text: Sam, a Permanent Resident of Canada, was convicted for shoplifting... Which of the following is the best answer?

options.a: Since her conviction amounts to criminality, there will not be any adverse immigration consequences for her.
options.b: Since her conviction amounts to criminality, she will be referred to the Immigration Division for a removal hearing. If she loses that hearing, she has the right to appeal to IAD.
options.c: Since her conviction amounts to criminality, she will be referred to the Immigration Division for a removal hearing. If she loses that hearing, she does not have the right to appeal to IAD.
options.d: Since her conviction amounts to criminality, an immigration officer will issue a deportation order for her...

explanation: Because she was imprisoned for only ten days (under the 6-month threshold for serious criminality), she retains the right to appeal to the IAD.

text: The IRB's guidelines define Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) as:

options.a: A meeting called by the Immigration Appeal Division (IAD) when it believes there is a chance that Sponsor's appeal could be resolved without an oral hearing.
options.b: A conference to determine Sponsor's eligibility to appeal his/her Sponsorship Application refusal.
options.c: A meeting called by the Immigration Appeal Division (IAD) when it believes there is a chance that Sponsor's appeal could be easily resolved...
options.d: A conference to determine Sponsor's eligibility... impaired due to physical, sexual, mental or psychological abuse.

explanation: An ADR is an informal meeting between you, the Minister's Counsel and an ERO...

text: What is the time limit for disclosure of the witness list and other evidence before the Refugee Protection Division (RPD) hearing date?

options.a: 15 days
options.b: 10 days
options.c: 7 days
options.d: 21 days

explanation: The RPD must get your evidence as early as possible or no later than 10 days before your hearing.

text: Which government institutions are involved with the refugee claim process?

options.a: CBSA
options.b: IRB
options.c: IRCC
options.d: All of the above

explanation: CBSA handles claims at POE, IRB decides protection, IRCC manages inland claims.

text: Which of the following statements is true?

options.a: Examples of persecution on the basis of race include...
options.b: Foreign nationals who are forced to flee their home countries due to natural disasters qualify for protection...
options.c: A refugee claimant must have experienced persecution in their home country to comply with the definition...
options.d: A person who has been granted the status of Convention Refugee or person in need of protection may lose such status if the circumstances in the country from whom the claimant sought protection no longer exist

explanation: Cessation of refugee protection occurs if the reasons for which the person sought refugee protection have ceased to exist.

text: Which one of the following parties is least likely to attend a refugee hearing?

options.a: Claimant and their Counsel
options.b: Interpreter
options.c: Presiding Member
options.d: Minister's Counsel

explanation: The Minister's counsel usually participates only if they don't agree with your claim or file a Notice of Intent to Intervene.

text: Which of the following is NOT TRUE when it comes to establishing the elements of a refugee claim under convention grounds?

options.a: To lead and coordinate international action to protect refugees and resolve refugee problems worldwide
options.b: To assess each refugee's situation and refer those cases to IRCC for which resettlement is the only durable solution
options.c: To assist those in need with their basic necessities such as food, water, and shelter in emergency situations
options.d: To approve claims for refugee protection in Canada, based on the 1951 Convention and the 1967 Protocol Relating to the Status of Refugees

explanation: This role belongs to the UNHCR... not Canadian authorities.

text: Which of the following is NOT TRUE when it comes to establishing the elements of a refugee claim under convention grounds?

options.a: The fear faced by the claimant is well-founded in both objective and subjective components
options.b: The claimant has suffered indecencies in the past but such treatment is unlikely to continue based on the current country conditions
options.c: The reason for the persecution is because of their activities to defend the right to free speech
options.d: The refugee claimant must establish to the RPD their identity and affiliation...

explanation: The definition of Convention refugee is forward-looking.

text: Maria delivered her third child in Canada 3 months ago... What is the timeline that she must submit her completed Basis of Claim form?

options.a: Within 7 days after her interview at the Port of Entry (POE).
options.b: Within 15 days after her interview at the Port of Entry (POE).
options.c: Within 30 days after her interview at the Port of Entry (POE).
options.d: There is no time limit as she is claiming asylum in an IRCC office, or 90 days on portal.

explanation: Once you start your application on the IRCC portal for inland claims, you have 90 days to give your answers and send your documents.

text: A very anxious client comes to your office... Which of the following approaches is most appropriate in supporting a client in emotional distress?

options.a: You tell her that there is nothing to be afraid of with you...
options.b: You try to be empathetic and not rush into conclusions or into explaining the refugee process to her. You courteously ask her if it is OK if you take notes, maintain eye contact with her, and show her your attention.
options.c: You begin by explaining the details about a refugee process in Canada...
options.d: Unsure

text: What elements should you, as representative, assist your clients demonstrate to rebut the possibility of an Internal Flight Alternative (IFA) within their country?

options.d: That there is still a serious possibility of the claimants to be persecuted in the proposed city or cities... and the undue hardship associated with the proposed relocation.

explanation: Both prongs must be satisfied (no serious possibility of persecution + not unreasonable/undue hardship to relocate).

text: What is meant by 'Vacation of Refugee Protection'?

options.a: Refugees can travel to any country except for the country against which they have received protection
options.b: The Refugee Protection Division (RPD) may refuse a claim if the claimant has left Canada
options.c: The Refugee Protection Division (RPD) may cancel a positive refugee decision based on misrepresentation.
options.d: The Refugee Protection Division (RPD) may cancel a positive refugee decision if the refugee travels to their country of nationality.

explanation: The Minister may make an application to the RPD to vacate a positive decision where a protected person obtained that decision by directly or indirectly misrepresenting or withholding material facts.

text: At the beginning of a Refugee Protection Division (RPD) hearing, the Member asks for verification of documents. What documents should be verified by the refugee claimant? Select all that apply.

options.a: IRCC application package
options.b: BOC form
options.c: Appointment of a Designated Representative for Accompanied Minors
options.d: It depends on what Board Member asks

explanation: Make sure you have all your documents with you, including: your Basis of Claim (BOC) form, your Notice to Appear, the originals and a copy of all evidence...

text: For the purpose of filing an appeal of a rejected refugee claim at the Refugee Protection Division (RPD), a refugee may appeal a negative decision by filing a completed Notice of Appeal to the RPD within what timeframe?

options.a: No later than 30 calendar days from the date of the hearing
options.b: No later than 15 days from the date a refugee claimant received the written reasons for the RPD decision
options.c: No later than 45 days from the date a refugee claimant received the written reasons for the RPD decision
options.d: No later than 30 days from the date of hearing...

text: Which of the following is the most correct statement regarding an RCIC's ability to submit new evidence to the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) after submitting the Appellant's Record?

options.a: An RCIC is unable to submit the new evidence to RAD after submitting the Appellant's Record (unless it arose after rejection or wasn't reasonably available).
options.b: An RCIC is able to submit the new evidence to RAD within 10 days...
options.c: An RCIC is unable to submit the new evidence to RAD... only if minister counsel intervened.
options.d: An RCIC is able to submit the new evidence to RAD... if minister counsel intervened.

explanation: On appeal, the person may present only evidence that arose after the rejection of their claim or that was not reasonably available at the time of rejection.

text: The Appellant's Record must contain which of the following documents? i. The notice of decision... ii. All or part of the transcript... iii. Any documents that the RPD refused to accept...

options.a: i and ii
options.b: i and iii
options.c: i, ii, and iii
options.d: only i

explanation: All must be submitted.

text: Mr. Kaur's refugee protection application was rejected by the RPD member alleging that the application has no credible basis or is manifestly unfounded. As his next step, Mr. Kaur can appeal the RPD decision to the RAD.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

explanation: You cannot appeal the RPD decision if it says your claim has no credible basis or is manifestly unfounded.

text: Which of the following statements is NOT TRUE about re-opening an appeal?

options.a: An application may be made to re-open a decided or abandoned appeal at any time before a federal court makes a final determination.
options.b: Once an appeal is refused, it cannot be re-opened. The appellant can make an application for leave to apply for judicial review.
options.c: A refused appeal cannot be opened unless it is established that there was a failure to observe a principle of natural justice.
options.d: The division may allow the application to re-open if it is established that the counsel provided inadequate representation in the proceedings.

text: For the purpose of filing an appeal at the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD), the responsibilities of the appellant or their Counsel's are to:

options.a: Provide three copies of the Notice of Appeal... no later than 15 days...
options.b: Provide two copies of the appellant's record... no later than 45 days...
options.c: Make sure that all of the documents you provide are in the right format...
options.d: All of the above

text: What is the maximum time allowed to file an appeal to the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) against a decision of the Refugee Protection Division?

options.a: 15 days after the date of the decision
options.b: 15 days after the day written reasons for the decision are received
options.c: 25 days after the date of the Notice of Decision
options.d: 15 days after the day the Decision is mailed out

text: What is the time limit for Perfecting an Appeal to the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD)?

options.a: 45 days after the appeal is filed
options.b: 45 days after the day written reasons for the decision are received
options.c: 30 days after the appeal is filed
options.d: 30 days after the day written reasons for the decision are received

text: If an appeal of a Refugee Protection Division (RPD) decision cannot be file within the time limit set out in the IRPR, or perfected within the time limit set out in the IRPR, the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) may....

options.a: For reasons of fairness and natural justice, extend each of those time limits by the number of days that is necessary in the circumstances
options.b: For reasons of fairness and natural justice, extend the time limit for perfecting the appeal...
options.c: For reasons of fairness and natural justice, extend the time limit for filing the appeal...
options.d: Not extend the time limits for the filing of or perfecting the Appeal.

text: Which of the following is NOT a factor specified under 67(5) of the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) Rules, to be considered in an application to change date or time of a hearing?

options.a: when the party made the application
options.b: the time the party has had to prepare for the hearing
options.c: the efforts made by the party to be ready to start or continue the hearing
options.d: in the case of a party who requests more time to obtain information in support of their arguments, the Division's ability to proceed in the absence of that information without causing an injustice
options.e: the country of origin of the party
options.g: the knowledge and experience of any counsel who represents the party

explanation: The rules specify factors like timing of the application, efforts made, time to prepare... Country of origin is not a procedural factor...

text: The Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) does not have jurisdiction to reopen on any ground-including a failure to observe a principle of natural justice - an appeal in respect of which the Federal Court has made a final determination.

options.a: True
options.b: False
options.c: Unsure

explanation: Section 171.1 explicitly prevents the RAD from reopening an appeal on any ground once the Federal Court has made a final determination.

text: Within how many hours of detention must the initial ID review occur?

options.a: 24 hours
options.b: 48 hours
options.c: 72 hours
options.d: One week

explanation: The Immigration Division (ID) must review the reasons for detention within 48 hours of detention, or as soon as possible afterwards.

text: What is the standard of proof for determining the credibility of an applicant's narrative in immigration applications?

options.a: Beyond a reasonable doubt
options.b: Preponderance of evidence
options.c: Clear and convincing evidence
options.d: Balance of probabilities greater than 50%

explanation: Credibility determinations are made on a balance of probabilities, meaning greater than 50% likelihood.

text: How should officers handle gaps or inconsistencies in an applicant's narrative?

options.a: Automatically reject the application
options.b: Ignore them as irrelevant
options.c: Identify and assess them in the context of the whole application
options.d: Use them as the sole basis for a decision

explanation: Officers should identify inconsistencies but assess them in the context of the entire application, not use them as the sole decision basis.

text: What is the threshold for establishing a 'reasonable chance' of persecution under section A96?

options.a: More than 50% likelihood
options.b: Less than 50% likelihood or a serious possibility
options.c: Absolute certainty
options.d: Mere possibility

explanation: The threshold for 'reasonable chance' is less than 50% likelihood, interpreted as a serious possibility.

text: What does the presumption of truthfulness imply in the context of an applicant's statement?

options.a: All statements are true under all circumstances
options.b: Creates a presumption of truth unless there's a reason to doubt
options.c: Only statements with direct evidence are considered true
options.d: Applicant's beliefs are always considered true

explanation: When an applicant states certain facts are true, it creates a presumption of truthfulness unless there is valid reason to doubt it.

text: How should credibility findings be approached when the applicant lacks personal documents?

options.a: Applicant should be found credible if all available evidence is considered and general credibility is established
options.b: Lack of personal documents should lead to automatic rejection
options.c: Credibility is only established with corroborating documents
options.d: Focus should be on microscopic examination of all details

explanation: In the absence of documents, if all available evidence is considered and the applicant's general credibility is established, they should be found credible.

text: When can an officer make a finding of a general lack of credibility?

options.a: For any minor inconsistencies in the narrative
options.b: When major discrepancies cast doubt on the totality of relevant statements
options.c: Based on the applicant's demeanor alone
options.d: Only if corroborating evidence is absent

explanation: A general lack of credibility may be found when significant contradictions or implausible evidence casts serious doubt on the overall narrative.

text: What should be considered when assessing minor inconsistencies in an applicant's narrative?

options.a: They should always lead to a negative credibility finding
options.b: They can be overlooked if other evidence supports the narrative
options.c: Only documentary evidence can override minor inconsistencies
options.d: They should be the sole basis for decision-making

explanation: Minor inconsistencies do not automatically result in a negative credibility finding, nor should they be the sole decision-making basis.

text: What is the role of an applicant's demeanor in credibility assessments?

options.a: It is the primary factor in assessing credibility
options.b: Demeanor should not be the sole measure of credibility
options.c: Demeanor is irrelevant in credibility assessments
options.d: Only positive demeanor leads to a positive credibility finding

explanation: The demeanor of the applicant is an unreliable measure and should not be the only factor in assessing credibility.

text: How should implausibility findings be approached in credibility assessments?

options.a: Any implausible statement leads to a negative finding
options.b: They require clear evidence supporting the inference of untruthfulness
options.c: Implausibility findings are based on the officer's personal opinion
options.d: They are not considered in credibility assessments

explanation: Implausibility findings should be limited to situations where the facts clearly support an inference that the applicant was not truthful.

text: What is required when addressing specific credibility concerns with an applicant?

options.a: The applicant should be given no opportunity to respond
options.b: Concerns should be addressed only after decision-making
options.c: Applicants must be given a chance to respond to concerns
options.d: Concerns are to be addressed internally without involving the applicant

explanation: Applicants should be provided an opportunity to respond to concerns about inconsistencies, either through interviews or procedural fairness letters.

text: Who may request an admissibility hearing for foreign nationals or permanent residents?

options.a: Immigration and Refugee Board of Canada (IRB)
options.b: Citizenship and Immigration Canada (IRCC)
options.c: Federal Court
options.d: Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA)

explanation: The CBSA may request an admissibility hearing if they believe someone has contravened the IRPA.

text: If continued detention is ordered, when will the next hearing before the ID occur?

options.a: The same day
options.b: Within seven days of the first review
options.c: After 30 days
options.d: When the detainee requests

explanation: If continued detention is ordered, the next hearing before the ID must occur within seven days of the first review.

text: How often must the ID hold hearings if a person remains detained?

options.a: Every 15 days
options.b: Once a week
options.c: At least once every 30 days
options.d: Only when the detainee requests

explanation: The Immigration Division holds further hearings at least once every 30 days for as long as the person is detained.

text: When can a detained person ask for an early review of detention?

options.a: Only after 30 days
options.b: At any time, if they present new facts
options.c: Within the first 48 hours
options.d: After the second hearing

explanation: A detained person may ask for an early review of detention at any time, but must present new facts to justify the request.

text: Who may ask the Federal Court for a judicial review of any IRB decision on detention?

options.a: Only the detained person
options.b: Only Minister's counsel
options.c: Any member of the public
options.d: Either the detained person or Minister's counsel

explanation: Either the detained person or Minister's counsel may ask the Federal Court for leave for a judicial review of any IRB decision on detention.

text: If a client is detained for immigration reasons, what is the first action an RCIC should take?

options.a: Prepare for the initial detention review within 48 hours
options.b: Request immediate release without a review
options.c: Appeal to the Federal Court
options.d: Wait for the CBSA to release the client

explanation: An RCIC should promptly prepare for the initial detention review, which is to occur within 48 hours of detention.

text: What should an RCIC do if their client's detention is extended after the first review?

options.a: Prepare for the next hearing within seven days
options.b: Guarantee the client's release with a bond
options.c: Apply for the client's citizenship
options.d: Reschedule the initial review

explanation: The RCIC should prepare for a follow-up hearing, which will be within seven days of the first review if detention is extended.

text: If a client believes their detention review was unfair, what can an RCIC advise them to do?

options.a: Ignore the decision
options.b: Seek leave for a judicial review from the Federal Court
options.c: Attempt to escape from detention
options.d: Directly contact the Minister's counsel

explanation: An RCIC should advise seeking leave for a judicial review of the IRB decision from the Federal Court if the client believes the process was unfair.

text: What action should an RCIC take if a detained client has new facts to present?

options.a: Request an early review of detention
options.b: Wait for the 30-day review period to pass
options.c: Advise the client to accept the detention
options.d: Negotiate with the CBSA officer

explanation: An RCIC should request an early review of detention if the client has new facts that may affect the outcome of the review.

text: How can an RCIC assist a client who is ordered to be released with conditions?

options.a: Challenge the conditions of release
options.b: Help the client understand and comply with the terms, such as reporting regularly
options.c: Pay the bond for the client
options.d: Arrange for immediate deportation

explanation: An RCIC should assist the client in understanding and adhering to the release conditions, such as ensuring they report as required.

text: By what day must a notice of appeal to the RAD be filed after receiving the written reasons for the RPD decision?

options.a: Within 10 business days
options.b: No later than 15 calendar days
options.c: Within 30 calendar days
options.d: No later than 45 calendar days

explanation: A notice of appeal must be filed to the RAD no later than 15 calendar days after receiving the written reasons for the RPD decision.

text: How long after receiving the RPD decision does one have to file the appellant's record?

options.a: No more than 45 days
options.b: Exactly 15 days
options.c: Within 60 days
options.d: Before 30 days

explanation: The time limit is not 15 days, 60 days, or before 30 days, but specifically no more than 45 days.

text: What is the waiting period before the RAD makes a decision if no hearing is ordered?

options.a: 10 days
options.b: 15 days
options.c: 30 days
options.d: 45 days

explanation: The waiting period before the RAD makes a decision is not 10 days, 30 days, or 45 days, but specifically 15 days if no hearing is ordered.

text: What must be done if a person misses the deadline to file the notice of appeal or the appellant's record?

options.a: Appeal to the Federal Court immediately
options.b: File an application for an extension of time
options.c: Wait for the RAD to issue a new deadline
options.d: Start the entire refugee claim process over

explanation: If the deadline is missed, one must file an application for an extension of time, following specific RAD Rules.

text: What action does the RAD take after the Minister decides to intervene and before making a final decision on the appeal?

options.a: Makes an immediate decision without waiting
options.b: Dismisses the appeal
options.c: Waits 15 days for a reply to the Minister and the RAD
options.d: Waits 30 days for public commentary

explanation: After the Minister decides to intervene, the RAD waits 15 days for the person to reply to the Minister and the RAD before making a decision.

text: What will you receive from the RAD when a decision is made on your refugee appeal?

options.a: A verbal notification only
options.b: An email summary of the decision
options.c: A written Notice of Decision and an explanation of the reasons
options.d: A direct phone call from the RAD member

explanation: You will receive a written Notice of Decision from the RAD, along with an explanation of the reasons for the decision.

text: What happens if your refugee appeal is allowed by the RAD?

options.a: You are immediately deported
options.b: The RPD decision is confirmed
options.c: The RPD decision is set aside
options.d: Your case is sent back to the RPD

explanation: Being allowed does not mean immediate deportation, confirmation of the RPD decision, or that your case is sent back to the RPD.

text: Within how many days must IRCC or CBSA seek leave for judicial review if they disagree with a positive RAD decision?

options.a: 30 days
options.b: 15 days
options.c: 7 days
options.d: 60 days

explanation: If IRCC or CBSA disagrees with a positive decision, they must seek leave for judicial review within 15 days.

text: What status will you obtain if you receive a positive decision and the Minister does not seek leave within 15 days?

options.a: Temporary resident status
options.b: Protected person status
options.c: Permanent resident status
options.d: Refugee claimant status

explanation: If you receive a positive decision and the Minister does not seek leave within 15 days, you will get 'protected person' status.

text: What does the RPD do if the RAD refers your case back for a new hearing?

options.a: Dismisses your case automatically
options.b: Informs you of next steps, including the scheduling of a rehearing
options.c: Grants you permanent residence
options.d: Requires you to leave Canada immediately

explanation: If the RAD refers your case back to the RPD, the RPD will inform you of the next steps, including when the rehearing is scheduled.

text: When representing a client with disabilities in an IRB proceeding, what language approach should an RCIC encourage the client to use?

options.a: Technical legal terminology
options.b: The language and terminology the client prefers to describe their experience
options.c: A standardized set of terms for consistency
options.d: Simplified language to ease the process

explanation: The focus is not on using technical legal terms, standardized language, or simplified language, but on respecting the client's choice of language.

text: As an RCIC, how should you address misconceptions about disabilities when representing a client in IRB proceedings?

options.a: Focus solely on legal arguments, ignoring any misconceptions
options.b: Challenge myths and stereotypes about disabilities that may affect the client's case
options.c: Agree with any misconceptions to simplify the case
options.d: Avoid discussing disabilities altogether

explanation: An RCIC should actively challenge myths and stereotypes relating to disabilities to ensure fair representation of the client.

text: What strategy should an RCIC use to create a safe environment for a client during an IRB hearing?

options.a: Request the fastest possible resolution to minimize client stress
options.b: Ensure proceedings are conducted with sensitivity, considering the client's trauma and memory impact
options.c: Advise the client to avoid discussing traumatic experiences
options.d: Rely solely on documentary evidence, avoiding oral testimony

explanation: An RCIC should ensure that proceedings are conducted with sensitivity, especially considering the client's trauma and its impact on memory and ability to testify.

text: When requesting accommodations for a client, what is a crucial step for an RCIC?

options.a: Wait until the day of the hearing to request accommodations
options.b: Only request accommodations that are visible and apparent
options.c: Specify the required accommodation and provide it to all parties as early as possible
options.d: Assume the IRB will automatically provide necessary accommodations

explanation: An RCIC should specify the needed accommodations and provide this information to all parties as early as possible.

text: Why should IRB members use the same terminology as the person appearing before them?

options.a: To standardize the language used in proceedings
options.b: To respect the person's choice to describe their own experience
options.c: To simplify the legal process
options.d: To ensure clarity in communication

explanation: The reason is not standardization, simplification, or clarity, but respect for the individual's language choice.

text: What is a misconception about people with disabilities according to the IRB guidelines?

options.a: That disabilities always require special accommodations
options.b: That a physical disability necessarily has some bearing on intellectual ability
options.c: That all disabilities are visible
options.d: That disabilities prevent effective communication

explanation: The misconception is not about the need for accommodations, visibility, or communication abilities, but about the correlation between physical and intellectual abilities.

text: What principles should be applied by all engaged in the adjudication process?

options.a: Strict adherence to legal procedures without exception
options.b: Prioritizing efficiency over the comfort of participants
options.c: Leading proceedings with sensitivity, anticipating trauma's impact on testimony, and creating a safe environment
options.d: Focusing solely on the legal aspects of the case

explanation: Principles include leading with sensitivity, anticipating trauma's impact, and creating a safe environment for testimony.

text: What accommodations must the IRB provide to individuals with disabilities or vulnerabilities?

options.a: Only accommodations requested by legal counsel
options.b: No accommodations unless explicitly required by law
options.c: Reasonable accommodations considering disability, vulnerability, or personal characteristics
options.d: Accommodations only for physical disabilities

explanation: The IRB must provide reasonable accommodations for disabilities, vulnerabilities, or personal characteristics.

text: How can the IRB accommodate a person's disability, vulnerability, or personal characteristics?

options.a: By only providing interpreters
options.b: By limiting the duration of hearings
options.c: Various means, including priority scheduling, alternative hearing schedules, support persons, and informal settings
options.d: By automatically ruling in favor of vulnerable persons

explanation: Accommodations include priority scheduling, alternative hearing schedules, support persons, and creating informal settings.

text: How should an RCIC prepare a client for an ADR conference in an IRB proceeding?

options.a: Advise the client to agree to whatever is proposed for a quick resolution
options.b: Prepare only legal arguments without reviewing the appeal file
options.c: Review the appeal file thoroughly and prepare to discuss any special circumstances in the case
options.d: Focus only on the weaknesses in the Minister's counsel's case

explanation: An RCIC should thoroughly review the appeal file with the client and prepare them to discuss special circumstances or any mistakes made in the original decision.

text: What is the purpose of an Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) conference in the appeal process?

options.a: To present new evidence to the IAD
options.b: To train appellants for the hearing
options.c: To discuss the case and encourage both sides to agree on a decision without a hearing
options.d: To allow the appellant to negotiate a lesser penalty

explanation: An ADR conference is an informal meeting to discuss the case and encourage agreement on a decision without needing a hearing.

text: What is the deadline for filing a Notice of Appeal after receiving a removal order?

options.a: 15 days from the date of receiving the removal order
options.b: 30 days from the date of receiving the removal order
options.c: 45 days from the date of receiving the removal order
options.d: 60 days from the date of receiving the removal order

explanation: The filing deadline is not 15 days, 45 days, or 60 days, but specifically 30 days from the date of receiving the removal order.

text: What does 'authorized counsel' mean in the context of an immigration appeal?

options.a: Any lawyer regardless of their membership in a professional organization
options.b: Counsel who belongs to a Canadian provincial law society, the Chambre des notaires du Québec, or the CICC
options.c: A representative appointed by the government
options.d: Any person willing to represent someone, regardless of their qualifications

explanation: 'Authorized counsel' refers to counsel who are members of a Canadian provincial law society, the Chambre des notaires du Québec, or the College of Immigration and Citizenship Consultants.

text: What should a person do if their appeal representative is unpaid?

options.a: Nothing, as unpaid counsel does not need to be declared
options.b: Pay them a nominal fee to make them 'paid counsel'
options.c: Complete and send a Notice of Representation without a Fee or Other Consideration
options.d: Inform the IAD verbally at the hearing

explanation: For an unpaid counsel, the client must complete and send a Notice of Representation without a Fee or Other Consideration to the IAD.

text: Who can be appointed as a designated representative for a child or a person unable to understand the hearing?

options.a: Any family member, regardless of their understanding of the case
options.b: A person responsible for making decisions in the best interests of the appellant
options.c: An IAD member
options.d: A government-appointed legal expert

explanation: A designated representative is someone responsible for making decisions in the best interests of the appellant, such as a child or a person unable to understand the hearing.

text: What accommodations might be made for a 'vulnerable person' in the appeal process?

options.a: Automatic approval of their appeal
options.b: An extension of all deadlines by 60 days
options.c: Receiving changes in procedures or special help due to trauma, illness, or other difficulties
options.d: A waiver of all legal and administrative fees

explanation: Vulnerable persons may receive accommodations like changes in procedures or special help due to their situation, like trauma or illness.

text: Who represents the Minister in an immigration appeal?

options.a: A member of the Immigration and Refugee Board
options.b: An independent legal advisor
options.c: An officer from the Canada Border Services Agency
options.d: A representative from Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada

explanation: The Minister is represented by an officer from the Canada Border Services Agency in an immigration appeal.

text: What is the role of an Early Resolution Officer (ERO) in the appeal process?

options.a: To represent the appellant in the hearing
options.b: To give information to help prepare the case, but not provide legal advice
options.c: To make the final decision on the appeal
options.d: To act as a mediator between the appellant and the Minister

explanation: EROs do not represent appellants, make final decisions on appeals, or act as mediators.

text: What is the deadline for sending the disclosure to the IAD after receiving the appeal record?

options.a: Within 30 days of receiving the appeal record
options.b: No later than 60 days after receiving the appeal record
options.c: Before the hearing date
options.d: Within 15 days of the hearing date

explanation: The deadline is not within 30 days, before the hearing date, or within 15 days of the hearing, but specifically 60 days after receiving the appeal record.

text: What happens if you do not provide disclosure or inform the IAD of your intention not to provide documents?

options.a: The hearing will be postponed
options.b: The appeal will be automatically approved
options.c: The IAD can dismiss your appeal or declare it abandoned
options.d: The IAD will request documents directly from the Minister

explanation: If you do not send disclosure or inform the IAD of your intention not to provide documents, the IAD can dismiss your appeal or declare it abandoned.

text: Who is eligible to appeal a removal order to the Immigration Appeal Division (IAD)?

options.a: Any foreign national
options.b: Permanent residents, foreign nationals with a permanent resident visa, Convention Refugees or Protected Persons
options.c: Tourists and temporary workers
options.d: Anyone who has been in Canada for more than a year

explanation: Eligibility for appealing a removal order to the IAD includes permanent residents, foreign nationals with a permanent resident visa, Convention Refugees, or Protected Persons.

text: What is the deadline for filing a Notice of Appeal to the IAD after receiving a removal order?

options.a: 15 days from the date of receiving the removal order
options.b: 30 days from the date of receiving the removal order
options.c: 60 days from the date of the decision
options.d: 90 days from the date of the decision

explanation: The deadline to file a Notice of Appeal to the IAD is 30 days from the date of receiving the removal order.

text: Who cannot appeal a removal order to the IAD?

options.a: Those who have overstayed their visa
options.b: Those found inadmissible for reasons like serious criminality, security threats, or violation of human rights
options.c: All foreign nationals
options.d: Permanent residents regardless of the reason

explanation: Individuals cannot appeal if they were found inadmissible due to serious criminality, security threats, organized crime involvement, or human rights violations.

text: What must be included when starting an appeal to the IAD against a removal order?

options.a: Just a verbal statement of intent to appeal
options.b: A completed Notice of Appeal form and a copy of the removal order
options.c: A letter of recommendation from a legal representative
options.d: Payment for the appeal process

explanation: To start an appeal, one must submit a completed Notice of Appeal form and a copy of the removal order.

text: What happens if you or your counsel do not respond to a communication from the IAD?

options.a: The IAD will automatically rule in your favor
options.b: The IAD will extend the deadline
options.c: The IAD can dismiss your appeal or declare it abandoned
options.d: The IAD will pause the process until you respond

explanation: If there is no response to a communication from the IAD, they can dismiss the appeal or declare it abandoned, ending the process.

text: What is an 'Accompanied minor' according to Chairperson’s Guideline 3?

options.a: A minor who arrives in Canada with a parent or legal guardian or reunites with them in Canada.
options.b: A minor who is alone in Canada without parents or legal guardians.
options.c: A minor separated from parents but with other adult family members.
options.d: A minor who is under the care of a contracted DR.

explanation: An accompanied minor is defined as one who arrives in Canada at the same time as or reunites with one or more parents or legal guardians.

text: What is required of a Designated Representative (DR) for a minor?

options.a: Must be a legal guardian or parent of the minor.
options.b: Must be 18 or older, understand the proceedings, act in the minor's best interests, and have no conflicting interests.
options.c: Must be appointed by the IRB under contract.
options.d: Must be a family member or friend of the minor.

explanation: A DR must meet specific criteria including age, understanding, willingness, and lack of conflicting interests.

text: When should a DR be replaced according to the guideline?

options.a: After two years of service.
options.b: If the DR moves out of the country.
options.c: If the minor reaches the age of 18.
options.d: If there is a conflict of interest or inability to act in the minor's best interests.

explanation: A DR must be replaced if there’s a conflict of interest or they cannot act in the minor’s best interest.

text: What are the factors to consider when deciding to question a minor in a hearing?

options.a: Minor's educational background and language skills.
options.b: Age and maturity, cultural norms, trauma experiences, desire to testify, and parties' views.
options.c: Duration of stay in Canada and legal status.
options.d: Parental consent and minor's physical health.

explanation: Factors include age, maturity, cultural norms, trauma, desire to testify, and parties' opinions.

text: How should credibility issues be addressed with minors during hearings?

options.a: By directly challenging the minor's statements.
options.b: Using technical legal terminology to test understanding.
options.c: Asking for evidence or witnesses to support their statements.
options.d: Using plain language and a non-confrontational tone, asking for clarification on discrepancies.

explanation: Addressing credibility involves plain language, a non-confrontational approach, and seeking clarification.

text: What is the primary focus of an RCIC when representing a minor in immigration proceedings?

options.a: Ensuring the best interests of the minor are the primary consideration.
options.b: Prioritizing the completion of the proceedings as quickly as possible.
options.c: Aligning with the minor's parents' or guardians' interests.
options.d: Minimizing the involvement of the minor in the proceedings.

explanation: The RCIC should ensure the minor's best interests are at the forefront throughout the proceedings.

text: How should an RCIC address the communication needs of a minor during the proceedings?

options.a: Using formal legal language to ensure accuracy.
options.b: Adapting communication to suit the minor's age, maturity, and cultural background.
options.c: Relying on written communication to avoid misunderstandings.
options.d: Speaking to the minor the same way as to an adult.

explanation: Effective communication with a minor involves adapting to their developmental level and cultural context.

text: What is crucial for an RCIC to establish when representing a minor who has experienced trauma?

options.a: Focusing solely on legal aspects and avoiding personal matters.
options.b: Encouraging the minor to avoid discussing traumatic experiences.
options.c: Creating a safe and trusting environment for the minor.
options.d: Urging the minor to provide detailed accounts of their experiences.

explanation: It's important to establish a safe space for minors, especially those who have experienced trauma, to build trust.

text: In what scenario should an RCIC consider seeking a change of the Designated Representative for the minor?

options.a: Only when the minor requests it.
options.b: If the minor has been in the proceedings for more than a year.
options.c: When the DR is not fluent in the minor's language.
options.d: When there's a conflict of interest or the DR is unable to act in the minor's best interests.

explanation: An RCIC should consider seeking a new DR if there’s a conflict of interest or the current DR cannot act in the minor’s best interests.

text: How should an RCIC approach the issue of credibility when representing a minor?

options.a: Directly challenging any inconsistencies in the minor’s statements.
options.b: Asking the minor to provide physical evidence for their statements.
options.c: Using a gentle and non-confrontational approach to help the minor clarify any discrepancies.
options.d: Insisting on a consistent narrative, regardless of the minor’s comfort level.

explanation: A gentle approach in addressing credibility helps the minor feel comfortable in clarifying any inconsistencies.

text: What is the IRB's commitment regarding the impact of trauma on access to justice?

options.a: To ensure all trauma-affected individuals receive priority in proceedings.
options.b: To provide legal representation to all individuals affected by trauma.
options.c: The IRB is committed to removing barriers created by the impact of trauma on access to justice.
options.d: To train all IRB members and staff in trauma counseling.

explanation: The IRB's commitment is to remove barriers to justice that arise due to the impact of trauma.

text: When should trauma-informed adjudication principles be applied in IRB proceedings?

options.a: Only when trauma is officially diagnosed by a medical professional.
options.b: In cases where trauma impacts a person's ability to fully participate, including disclosed trauma or trauma apparent in the record or proceedings.
options.c: Only in cases involving gender considerations.
options.d: At the discretion of the IRB members.
